channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.eurogamer.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.eurogamer.net

item.title: Mass Effect: Andromeda actor says the game "was done dirty" by EA and "a very toxic atmosphere" online
item.content:encoded:
"It hurt most because I knew that was it."

It's been over nine years since Mass Effect: Andromeda tried to kickstart a new era of BioWare's original sci-fi RPG universe, an experiment that ended with pretty poor results. Now, we're eagerly awaiting news on the mainline follow-up revealed in late 2020 and Amazon's TV show.

One of Andromeda's leads, Tom Taylorson, the voice actor behind Scott Ryder, recently had a chat with fan site We Are Mass Effect (hat tip to IGN). During the conversation, he reflected on what went wrong during the game's development and after its release, even if it was eventually reappraised by some fans of the franchise.

"It was done dirty by a publisher expecting too much from it, not being fully cooked, forced out the door too early, forced to use corporate's shiny new engine when many of the team didn't know how to work with it and it was NOT suited to the storytelling part of the game," he said. Almost a decade later, Taylorson isn't pulling any punches, underlining well-known problems during the game's bumpy development (such as the forced switch to DICE's Frostbite engine), and BioWare Montreal's lack of experience when it came to taking the lead on vast projects.

To be fair, and as also explained by IGN's report, 2024's Dragon Age: The Veilguard was also built on Frostbite and, for all its faults, was one of the most polished triple-A releases of that year. It's true BioWare had trouble adapting to it on Mass Effect: Andromeda and Anthem, though. It's worth noting, at this point, that the next Mass Effect has returned to Unreal Engine.

The actor also pointed out the online reaction to the title wasn't the kindest when the game finally launched: "It was released to a VERY toxic atmosphere online and elsewhere in the gaming space," he explains. The actor is convinced "online chuds" amplified the problems the game faced during its launch window: "Their love of hate sealed the deal. What saddens me is that this would not be the last time I was in a project doomed by online haters picking a game for Punching Bag of The Week: I also worked on Highguard."

The Mass Effect franchise is eventually going to return — though, at this point, anything could still happen with the next game — but the plans for Andromeda's own series were scrapped following the poor critical reception and disappointing sales. "It hurt most because I knew that was it — Ryder wouldn't be coming back... I, and others, thought we'd have a good decade of playing with these characters in these spaces. And just like that — gone."

Related topics


https://www.eurogamer.net/mass-effect-andromeda-actor-reflects-on-poor-reception
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Hong Kong-set ‘bodycam FPS’ Better Than Dead gets a release date
item.content:encoded:
A release date for body cam FPS Better Than Dead has been announced, as well as a new gameplay trailer packed with Hong Kong cinema-style action.

The game, developed by Monte Gallo, will release on the 12th May on Steam. This will be an Early Access release, with a note on the Steam page saying the developer will "improve and expand it with player feedback".

The game is planned to stay in this Early Access state for six-12 months. When it eventually leaves Early Access, it will have a more "hardcore" mode called ‘For Heaven’. The game will also receive some additional levels, and the game's "true ending" as the full launch hits. The Early Access release will have a "full campaign" however, so if this seems like your type of thing, you should be able to get a substantial package next month.

First announced last year, Better Than Dead is a photo-realistic shooter in a small-but-growing bodycam subgenre of FPS games. Better Than Dead’s peers include Bodycam, which popularised the new style of shooter thanks to a viral gameplay trailer.

Better Than Dead looks to be a gritty shooter in the same vein to Bodycam, however the upcoming title is set apart thanks to its classic Hong Kong action thriller theming, taking inspiration from directors like John Wu and Yuen Woo-ping.

A press release describes the game as a title “built around immediacy and tension” which strips away excess to focus on what matters: movement, reaction, and execution. It continues: “Encounters are short, unforgiving, and decisive. Mistakes are costly. Success is earned.”

Related topics


https://www.eurogamer.net/better-than-dead-release-date
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: The Last of Us Part 2's most shocking death was "controversial internally, too", former Naughty Dog developer admits
item.content:encoded:
There is one death in particular in The Last of Us Part 2 that many are still struggling to come to terms with, six years on from the game's release. And, it turns out that this death was not only controversial for those of us in The Last of Us' gaming community. Even many developers internally at Naughty Dog - the studio that made the game - questioned this decision.

Please be aware of major spoilers for The Last of Us Part 2 below.

Right, if you have read this far, you will likely know exactly which death I am talking about. In the opening hours of The Last of Us Part 2, the first game's protagonist Joel is beaten to death with a golf club by WLF member Abby.

YouTuber Kiwi Talkz recently asked former Naughty Dog artist Heather Cerlan if the studio knew that The Last of Us Part 2 - and Joel's death, specifically - was going to cause a bit of a stir among fans, and it absolutely did. "Oh yeah, most of the studio was like, 'really you're going to...'" (at this point, she dithered a bit, and stopped elaborating on what had been proposed to avoid spoilers, but of course we all know what she was talking about). "I think the studio was pretty split on the outcome of what happens," she soon continued, before admitting: "It was controversial internally, too."

Prior to The Last of Us Part 2's release, it leaked that Abby killed Joel, sparking a wave of online abuse and death threats aimed at both the character's actress Laura Bailey and her infant son. "Every time I went online, that's all I saw," Bailey revealed as part of the game's behind-the-scenes documentary, Grounded 2: Making The Last of Us Part 2. "Death threats and threats of violence."

Because of the online abuse surrounding The Last of Us Part 2, the star of Naughty Dog's next game received preparation for the role - and specifically the online reaction to it - from studio boss Neil Druckmann. Actress Tati Gabrielle revealed she had been given mental "bootcamp-ing" by Druckmann ahead of her turn as bounty hunter Jordan A Mun in Intergalactic: The Heretic Prophet. "[Druckmann] was like, 'Ignore it. No matter what, me and you, we're going to make something beautiful. We're going to make something that we're proud of'," Gabrielle said last year.

Intergalactic: The Heretic Prophet was first announced at the end of 2024, with a trailer featuring a retro futuristic energy that introduced Jordan A Mun. While we still don't know a huge amount about Naughty Dog's upcoming release, Druckmann revealed last March that Intergalactic: The Heretic Prophecy explores the topics of "faith and religion".

As for The Last of Us, last week actor Troy Baker told Eurogamer that he believes we will see Joel again, despite his death in Part 2.

Related topics


https://www.eurogamer.net/the-last-of-us-part-2-death-naughty-dog-controversial
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
